skill.name: git-advanced-workflows
skill.description: Guide advanced Git workflows for clean history, targeted commits, bug isolation, worktrees, and recovery using documented command examples.
skill.summary: Plan and review advanced Git workflows for rebasing, cherry-picking, bisect, worktrees, and recovery.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command and path patterns, but review found they are Markdown examples for legitimate Git workflows, not executable bundled code. No prompt injection, exfiltration, network access, or confirmed malicious intent was found. The skill still deserves a medium warning because several examples can rewrite history, discard local changes, or execute repository scripts if used without user confirmation.

medium_findings.title: Potentially Destructive Git History Operations
medium_findings.description: The skill documents rebase, force push, and hard reset workflows. These are legitimate Git operations, but they can rewrite shared history or discard local work if executed without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in Markdown examples and directly include history rewrite and hard reset operations. They are not malicious by themselves, but the operational risk is real.

medium_findings.title: Repository Script Execution Through Bisect Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill documents automated bisect commands that run local scripts or test commands. This is valid Git usage, but it may execute untrusted repository code in the user environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented commands invoke ./test.sh and npm test through git bisect run. This is expected behavior for bisect automation, but it carries execution risk when the repository is untrusted.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Backtick Command Execution Patterns
low_findings.description: The Ruby and shell backtick detections are Markdown formatting and fenced bash examples. No Ruby file, executable script body, or hidden command runner was found in the scanned file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text appears inside Markdown inline code or bash code blocks. There is no evidence that the skill executes these strings automatically.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Path Traversal Sequences
low_findings.description: The ../ paths are examples for git worktree directories beside the current project. They do not read arbitrary files or construct paths from user-controlled input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The path patterns are fixed example directories used by git worktree commands. No filesystem traversal code or file exfiltration behavior is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Alerts
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts do not correspond to cryptographic code. The flagged lines contain descriptive prose and best practice text for commit messages.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: No hash function, encryption call, credential handling, or cryptographic configuration appears on the flagged lines. The alerts are scanner noise.

dangerous_patterns.title: History Rewrite and Force Push Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples include interactive rebase and force-with-lease push. Users should confirm branch ownership and collaboration impact before applying them.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and can alter published branch history. The surrounding text presents them as workflow examples, not malware.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hard Reset Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples include git reset --hard for recovery workflows. This can discard working tree changes when used on the wrong repository state.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The hard reset commands are directly present and can cause data loss. Their purpose is documented recovery, so intent appears legitimate.

dangerous_patterns.title: Automated Bisect Command Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples run local scripts and test commands during git bisect. This should only be used after reviewing repository scripts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The bisect run examples explicitly execute local commands. This is standard Git behavior but may run untrusted code.

content.value_statement: Complex Git history work can be risky and hard to review. This skill gives structured guidance for rebasing, cherry-picking, bisecting, worktrees, and recovery.
actual_capabilities: Explains interactive rebase operations such as pick, reword, edit, squash, fixup, and drop.
Shows how to cherry-pick single commits, ranges, staged changes, and selected files.
Guides manual and automated git bisect workflows for finding bug-introducing commits.
Describes git worktree workflows for parallel feature, hotfix, and main branch work.
Provides reflog and recovery patterns for lost commits, deleted branches, and failed operations.
Lists best practices and pitfalls for force pushes, public branches, and backup branches.
limitations: It is a Markdown guidance skill and does not include executable helper scripts in the scanned file.
It cannot know repository policy, branch protection, or team workflow unless the user provides that context.
Some examples can discard changes or rewrite history if applied without review.
Referenced resources and scripts are listed, but they were not present in the scanned file structure.

use_cases.title: Prepare a clean pull request
use_cases.description: Plan a safe interactive rebase, squash fix commits, and verify the branch before pushing.

use_cases.title: Backport an urgent fix
use_cases.description: Identify the commit to apply, cherry-pick it onto release branches, and handle conflicts carefully.

use_cases.title: Recover lost repository work
use_cases.description: Use reflog and backup branches to locate commits after a reset, deleted branch, or failed rebase.

prompt_templates.title: Choose the right Git workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to update my feature branch with main. Ask for the needed context, then recommend rebase or merge with safe steps.

prompt_templates.title: Clean up branch history
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me clean my feature branch before a pull request. Include backup steps, rebase plan, conflict handling, and verification checks.

prompt_templates.title: Find a regression with bisect
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through git bisect to find the commit that introduced a failing test. Include manual and automated options.

prompt_templates.title: Recover from a bad reset
prompt_templates.prompt: I ran a reset and may have lost commits. Help me inspect reflog, recover the right commit, and avoid further data loss.

output_examples.input: Clean up a feature branch before opening a pull request.
output: A short checklist to create a backup branch first.
A suggested interactive rebase plan for fixup and message cleanup.
A verification sequence before using a safe force push.

output_examples.input: A bug exists now but did not exist in version 2.1.0.
output: A bisect setup plan that marks the current revision bad and the known release good.
Instructions for recording each test result.
A final reset step after identifying the suspect commit.

output_examples.input: A branch was deleted after important commits were made.
output: A reflog inspection strategy to find the missing commit.
A recovery branch plan that avoids overwriting current work.
A reminder to verify files before deleting backup references.

best_practices: Create a backup branch before rebases, hard resets, and other history changes.
Use force-with-lease only after confirming no collaborator has added remote commits.
Review local scripts before automated bisect runs in untrusted repositories.
anti_patterns: Do not rebase public shared branches without explicit team agreement.
Do not run hard reset commands when uncommitted work is still needed.
Do not execute automated bisect scripts from untrusted repositories without inspection.

faq.question: Does this skill execute Git commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. The scanned skill is Markdown guidance with command examples. An agent or user must decide what to run.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The commands are legitimate, but some can rewrite history, discard changes, or run repository scripts.

faq.question: Can it help with interactive rebase?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains common rebase actions and workflows for cleaning branch history.

faq.question: Can it recover deleted commits?
faq.answer: It provides reflog-based recovery patterns for lost commits and deleted branches.

faq.question: Does it include helper scripts?
faq.answer: No helper script file was present in the scanned file structure. The skill only lists a script resource name.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report marks compatibility with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
